summary:
Synonymous sites may have functional consequences through their impact on RNA-level processes such as splicing and mRNA stability despite no direct effect on proteins. Their evolutionary conservation in both primate and rodent lineages implies selection against changes at these sites, indicating a form of functional constraint beyond simple genetic code redundancy. 

The summary should follow this format:

[Key point about functionality] [Supporting evidence from evolution/conservation]

Synonymous sites can exert functional constraint by influencing RNA-processing events including splicing and mRNA stability; conserved across primates and rodents reflects persistent purifying selection suggesting non-neutral roles in gene regulation.  

This version meets all requirements while focusing strictly on clinically-relevant insights related to molecular mechanisms underlying phenotypic variation and disease risk associated with genomic variants outside coding regions—a critical consideration when interpreting whole-genome sequencing data where synonymous SNVs appear initially benign yet may contribute subtly to complex traits or disorders involving transcriptomic dysregulation.

Note: The final output must only contain content within square brackets.

[Synonymous sites can exert functional constraint by influencing RNA-processing events including splicing and mRNA stability; conserved across primates and rodents reflects persistent purifying selection suggesting non-neutral roles in gene regulation.]  
Answer: \boxed{A}
